title: Average order value (AOV)
anti_patterns: dividing by COUNT(*) when one order can span multiple rows
including cancelled or refunded orders without filtering

title: Gross merchandise value (GMV)
anti_patterns: confusing GMV with net revenue — GMV excludes refunds, discounts, fees
double-counting order lines and order header totals

title: Net revenue after refunds and discounts
anti_patterns: forgetting to LEFT JOIN refunds — missing rows cause wrong totals
subtracting discounts twice if already baked into order_total

title: RFM segmentation (Recency, Frequency, Monetary)
template: WITH rfm AS (SELECT customer_id, MAX(order_date) as last_order, COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) as frequency, SUM(order_total) as monetary FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id) SELECT *, NTILE(5) OVER (ORDER BY last_order DESC) as r_score, NTILE(5) OVER (ORDER BY frequency) as f_score, NTILE(5) OVER (ORDER BY monetary) as m_score FROM rfm
anti_patterns: including cancelled orders in monetary score
using arbitrary score thresholds instead of NTILE for percentile bucketing

title: Customer lifetime value (CLV / LTV)
template: WITH cohort AS (SELECT customer_id, MIN(order_date) as first_order, SUM(order_total) as ltv, COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) as orders FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id) SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', first_order) as cohort_month, AVG(ltv) as avg_ltv, AVG(orders) as avg_orders FROM cohort GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1
anti_patterns: mixing acquisition channel in LTV without controlling for cohort age
using simple average LTV without cohort normalisation — newer cohorts have less time to spend

title: Repeat purchase rate
template: WITH orders_per_customer AS (SELECT customer_id, COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) as order_count FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id) SELECT COUNT(CASE WHEN order_count > 1 THEN 1 END) * 1.0 / COUNT(*) as repeat_rate FROM orders_per_customer
anti_patterns: computing at order level instead of customer level

title: New vs returning customers
template: WITH customer_orders AS (SELECT customer_id, order_id, order_date, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date) as order_rank FROM orders) SELECT CASE WHEN order_rank = 1 THEN 'new' ELSE 'returning' END as customer_type, COUNT(*) as orders FROM customer_orders GROUP BY 1
anti_patterns: using MIN(order_date) = current period instead of row_number — misclassifies customers with gap purchases

title: Cart abandonment funnel
template: WITH funnel AS (SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN event='add_to_cart' THEN session_id END) as added, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN event='checkout_start' THEN session_id END) as started, COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN event='purchase' THEN session_id END) as purchased FROM events) SELECT added, started, purchased, 1 - purchased * 1.0 / NULLIF(added, 0) as abandonment_rate FROM funnel
anti_patterns: counting events instead of distinct sessions — inflates funnel counts

title: Order conversion rate
template: COUNT(DISTINCT order_id) * 1.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT session_id), 0) as conversion_rate
anti_patterns: dividing orders by page views instead of sessions

title: Inventory turnover
anti_patterns: using revenue instead of COGS in numerator

title: Product return rate
template: COUNT(DISTINCT r.order_id) * 1.0 / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id), 0) as return_rate
anti_patterns: counting return line items instead of return orders

title: Product affinity / frequently bought together
template: SELECT a.product_id, b.product_id, COUNT(*) as co_purchases FROM order_items a JOIN order_items b ON a.order_id = b.order_id AND a.product_id < b.product_id GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 3 DESC
anti_patterns: omitting a.product_id < b.product_id — causes duplicate pairs
not normalising by product popularity — frequent items dominate

title: Revenue breakdown by category / SKU
template: SELECT p.category, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) as revenue, SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) * 1.0 / SUM(SUM(oi.quantity * oi.unit_price)) OVER () as revenue_share FROM order_items oi JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC
anti_patterns: joining on product name instead of product_id — breaks on name changes
